news_summary: Students and volunteers are actively contributing to space exploration by developing innovative tools, supporting astronaut research, and aiding NASA in discovering thousands of new planets, fostering human thriving beyond Earth.
news_summary_fr: Les étudiants et les bénévoles contribuent activement à l'exploration spatiale en développant des outils innovants, en soutenant les recherches des astronautes et en aidant la NASA à découvrir des milliers de nouvelles planètes, favorisant ainsi l'épanouissement de l'humanité au-delà de la Terre.
news_summary_es: Los estudiantes y los voluntarios contribuyen activamente a la exploración espacial mediante el desarrollo de herramientas innovadoras, el apoyo a la investigación de los astronautas y la colaboración con la NASA en el descubrimiento de miles de nuevos planetas, lo que favorece el desarrollo de la humanidad más allá de la Tierra.

articles.title: Students build a “cosmic radio” to listen for dark matter
articles.summary: A group of undergraduate students pulled off something remarkable: they built their own dark matter detector and used it to probe one of physics’ biggest mysteries. Working with limited resources but plenty of creativity, they designed a stripped-down experiment to hunt for axions — hypothetical particles that could make up dark matter.
articles.content:
Modern cosmology is often associated with massive observatories, advanced instruments, and large international collaborations backed by significant funding. However, meaningful progress does not always require such scale. Even in the complex search for dark matter, smaller teams with creative approaches and institutional support can still make important contributions.

A recent study published in the Journal of Cosmology and Astroparticle Physics (JCAP) highlights this idea. A group of undergraduate students from the University of Hamburg designed and built a cavity detector to search for axions, which are among the leading candidates for dark matter. Despite working with limited resources, they were able to establish new experimental limits on axion properties, demonstrating that smaller experiments can still advance one of physics' biggest unresolved problems.

Student Funding and Institutional Support

The project was funded through a student research grant from the University of Hamburg, provided by the Hub for Crossdisciplinary Learning. This program supports independent research projects led by students.

"We were kind of embedded in the research group of the MADMAX dark matter experiment," explains Nabil Salama, one of the study's authors and a current M.Sc. student in Physics at the University of Hamburg. "MADMAX carries out a similar experiment on a much larger and more complex scale, and we benefited from their expertise and support."

"We are very grateful for this help," he adds, "and also to the University of Hamburg and the Quantum Universe Cluster of Excellence, which provided funding, access to key equipment such as the magnet, and invaluable support from researchers."

Building a Simple Detector to Search for Axions

"The benefit of working with dark matter, or axions, is that we expect it to be present everywhere in our galaxy," says Agit Akgümüs, the study's first author, who is pursuing an M.Sc. in Mathematical Physics at the University of Hamburg. "So essentially, no matter where you perform the experiment, you have some dark matter on your hand you can do experiments with."

Using their funding, the team assembled a compact experimental setup centered on a resonant cavity made from highly conductive materials. They also integrated the required electronics, cabling, structural supports, and measurement tools.

"The detector we built is essentially the simplest version of a cavity detector for dark matter," says Salama.

The students did not begin entirely from scratch. They made use of existing facilities, equipment, and guidance provided by the university and collaborating research groups. After construction, the system was carefully tested, calibrated, and operated to collect data.

"We reduced very complex experiments to their essential components," says Salama. "The result is a less sensitive setup, limited to a small search window, but still capable of producing new scientific data."

No Detection, but Important New Constraints

"The search for axions involves exploring a wide range of possible parameters," adds Akgümüs. "Our experiment covers only a small region, with limited sensitivity, but it still helps narrow down the possibilities. To actually find the particle, we need either much larger experiments or many different ones, each probing a specific region."

After completing their data collection, the team did not detect any signal that could be attributed to axions. However, this outcome still carries scientific value. It allows researchers to rule out the existence of axions with certain characteristics within the tested mass range, especially those that would interact more strongly with photons. By excluding these possibilities, the study helps refine the search and guide future experiments.

A Model for Scalable Dark Matter Experiments

"I think the point of our experiment is that things can be done on a smaller scale," says Salama. Akgümüs adds: "Our results are naturally more limited than those of larger experiments. Performance scales with resources and complexity. However, we have shown that it is possible to reduce these setups to a much smaller scale -- even to projects developed almost independently by students -- while still producing real scientific data."

During peer review, one referee made an especially notable observation, Salama recalls. The referee suggested that once the axion is discovered and its properties -- especially its mass -- are known, experiments like this could become much more accessible and might even be used in teaching laboratories.

"We were told that setups like ours could one day become standard student lab experiments," says Salama. "In a way, we may have anticipated that future, showing that it is already possible to build and operate such an experiment on a small scale."
articles.reasoning: The article describes a meaningful scientific achievement by undergraduate students who built a dark matter detector, contributing to the important and broad field of cosmology and particle physics. Their work advances the search for axions, a leading dark matter candidate, demonstrating that smaller-scale, resource-limited experiments can still produce valuable scientific data and help narrow down dark matter properties. This has broad significance for science and education, inspiring hope that future discoveries and accessible experiments are possible.
articles.personality_title: Students build a simple detector to help search for dark matter
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Dark matter is a mysterious substance that makes up much of the universe, but scientists have not yet found it. One idea is that dark matter could be made of particles called axions. Usually, searching for such particles requires big, expensive experiments.

**What happened** – A group of undergraduate students at the University of Hamburg built a small, simple device called a cavity detector to look for axions. They worked with limited money and equipment but got support from their university and a larger experiment called MADMAX. They designed, built, and tested their detector themselves.

**Impact** – Although the students did not find axions, their experiment helped rule out some possibilities about what axions might be like. This shows that even small experiments can add useful information to big scientific questions. It also proves that students can do important research with fewer resources.

**What's next step** – The team’s work suggests that future experiments like theirs could become common in teaching labs once axions are better understood. Larger experiments will continue the search, but smaller projects can help explore different parts of the problem.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Undergraduate students built a simple dark matter detector that helped narrow down where axions might be, showing small experiments can contribute to big science.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des étudiants construisent un détecteur simple pour chercher la matière noire
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La matière noire est une substance mystérieuse qui compose une grande partie de l'univers, mais les scientifiques ne l'ont pas encore trouvée. Une idée est que la matière noire pourrait être constituée de particules appelées axions. Habituellement, la recherche de ces particules nécessite de grandes expériences coûteuses.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Un groupe d'étudiants de premier cycle de l'Université de Hambourg a construit un petit dispositif simple appelé détecteur à cavité pour chercher les axions. Ils ont travaillé avec peu d'argent et d'équipement, mais ont reçu le soutien de leur université et d'une expérience plus grande nommée MADMAX. Ils ont conçu, construit et testé leur détecteur eux-mêmes.

**Impact** – Même si les étudiants n'ont pas trouvé d'axions, leur expérience a permis d'exclure certaines possibilités sur ce que les axions pourraient être. Cela montre que même de petites expériences peuvent apporter des informations utiles à de grandes questions scientifiques. Cela prouve aussi que les étudiants peuvent faire de la recherche importante avec peu de ressources.

**Prochaine étape** – Le travail de l'équipe suggère que des expériences comme la leur pourraient devenir courantes dans les laboratoires d'enseignement une fois que les axions seront mieux compris. Les grandes expériences continueront la recherche, mais les petits projets peuvent explorer différentes parties du problème.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Des étudiants de premier cycle ont construit un détecteur simple de matière noire qui a aidé à préciser où pourraient se trouver les axions, montrant que les petites expériences peuvent contribuer à la grande science.
articles.personality_title_es: Estudiantes construyen un detector simple para buscar materia oscura
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La materia oscura es una sustancia misteriosa que compone gran parte del universo, pero los científicos aún no la han encontrado. Una idea es que la materia oscura podría estar formada por partículas llamadas axiones. Normalmente, buscar estas partículas requiere experimentos grandes y costosos.

**Qué pasó** – Un grupo de estudiantes universitarios de la Universidad de Hamburgo construyó un dispositivo pequeño y simple llamado detector de cavidad para buscar axiones. Trabajaron con pocos recursos y equipo, pero recibieron apoyo de su universidad y de un experimento más grande llamado MADMAX. Ellos mismos diseñaron, construyeron y probaron su detector.

**Impacto** – Aunque los estudiantes no encontraron axiones, su experimento ayudó a descartar algunas posibilidades sobre cómo podrían ser los axiones. Esto muestra que incluso experimentos pequeños pueden aportar información útil a grandes preguntas científicas. También demuestra que los estudiantes pueden hacer investigaciones importantes con pocos recursos.

**Próximo paso** – El trabajo del equipo sugiere que experimentos como el suyo podrían convertirse en algo común en los laboratorios de enseñanza cuando se entiendan mejor los axiones. Los experimentos más grandes seguirán buscando, pero los proyectos pequeños pueden explorar diferentes partes del problema.

**Una frase clave** – Estudiantes universitarios construyeron un detector simple de materia oscura que ayudó a reducir dónde podrían estar los axiones, mostrando que los experimentos pequeños pueden contribuir a la ciencia grande.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a small, intricately crafted resonant cavity detector glowing softly inside a cozy university lab setting, surrounded by symbolic waves of radio signals gently rippling outward into a starry cosmic backdrop that hints at the vast, mysterious presence of dark matter in the galaxy.

articles.title: You Can Help Humans Thrive in Space
articles.summary: The second Artemis mission took four astronauts around the moon and back – the first crewed deep-space flight since 1972. Not everyone gets a chance to put on a space suit, but you can still be an important part of NASA’s human space exploration story by doing NASA science!
articles.content:
The second Artemis mission took four astronauts around the moon and back – the first crewed deep-space flight since 1972. Not everyone gets a chance to put on a space suit, but you can still be an important part of NASA’s human space exploration story by doing NASA science!

Volunteers with NASA’s citizen science projects have tested chili pepper plant varieties to grow in space, monitored active regions on the Sun, and analyzed data from experiments on how life adapts to the low-gravity, high-radiation environment of space. Participation does not require citizenship in any particular country – you only need a love of science and a desire to help. Join one of the projects below and help NASA make space travel safer and healthier.

Only a few minutes to spare? Space Umbrella is a great project for you. The brief online project tutorial will teach you how to read data collected by NASA’s Magnetosphere Multiscale (MMS) mission, which has been flying back and forth across Earth’s magnetosphere since 2015. By sorting data into in-magnetosphere and out-of-magnetosphere readings, you will help scientists learn more about how solar storms interact with our magnetosphere. Solar storms can pose a serious threat to astronauts, so this work can help missions minimize risks from radiation in space.

Are you a classroom teacher for students in grades 6-12? Through Growing Beyond Earth, middle and high school students and their teachers collaborate with Fairchild Botanical Garden scientists to grow candidate plants that are being evaluated as astronaut food. Today, on the International Space Station, astronauts tend to some of the same experimental leafy greens and hot pepper plants to unlock the secrets of how best to space farm terrestrial species. On really long missions, it won’t just be a question of easing the monotony of packaged/prepared foods – astronauts will have to grow their own food to supplement their diets. Sign up here to learn more.

Do you have some experience with data analysis? The Open Science Data Repository Analysis Working Groups need you to help analyze data from experiments about life in space. Join this international community of scientists, students, and everyone in between to help us understand how terrestrial life – from plants to mice and microbes to astronauts – responds to the space environment.

Into ham radio? Join the team called Ham Radio Science Citizen Investigation (HamSCI) and use your ham radio skills to deploy your own personal space weather station! These stations are designed to be relatively low cost and easy to build and deploy by science professionals, educational institutions, and citizen scientists (you!). Your observations will be aggregated into a central database to help answer questions about how the ionosphere responds to the Sun and the neutral atmosphere.

There are many more ways you can do NASA science. Check out all the current projects supported by NASA that need your help answering questions about our universe, solar system, and Earth.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights NASA's Artemis mission and promotes broad public participation in meaningful citizen science projects that support human space exploration. It emphasizes tangible benefits such as improving astronaut safety, advancing space farming, and understanding space environments, which have broad significance and inspire public engagement in space science.
articles.personality_title: How You Can Help NASA Explore Space Safely and Sustainably
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA recently completed its second Artemis mission, sending four astronauts around the moon and back. This was the first crewed deep-space flight in more than 50 years. While not everyone can go to space, NASA offers ways for people everywhere to contribute to space exploration through citizen science projects.

**What happened** – NASA invites volunteers to join projects that support human space travel. People have helped test chili pepper plants for growing in space, studied solar activity, and analyzed how life changes in low gravity and high radiation. These projects are open to anyone interested in science, no matter where they live.

**Impact** – These citizen science efforts help NASA solve real problems, like protecting astronauts from dangerous solar storms and figuring out how to grow food during long missions. For example, students can grow plants similar to those on the International Space Station, and ham radio enthusiasts can build space weather stations. These contributions improve safety and health for future space travelers.

**What's next step** – NASA encourages more people to join these projects. Whether you have a few minutes or special skills, there is a way to get involved. As more volunteers participate, NASA can gather better data to prepare for longer space missions and deeper exploration.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Anyone can support NASA’s space missions by joining citizen science projects that improve astronaut safety and help grow food in space.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment vous pouvez aider la NASA à explorer l’espace en toute sécurité et durabilité
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA a récemment mené sa deuxième mission Artemis, envoyant quatre astronautes autour de la Lune et retour. C’était le premier vol spatial habité en profondeur depuis plus de 50 ans. Même si tout le monde ne peut pas aller dans l’espace, la NASA propose des moyens pour que chacun puisse contribuer à l’exploration spatiale grâce à des projets de science participative.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – La NASA invite les volontaires à rejoindre des projets qui soutiennent les voyages spatiaux humains. Des personnes ont aidé à tester des plants de piment pour la culture dans l’espace, étudié l’activité solaire, et analysé comment la vie change en faible gravité et forte radiation. Ces projets sont ouverts à tous les passionnés de science, où qu’ils vivent.

**Impact** – Ces efforts de science participative aident la NASA à résoudre des problèmes réels, comme protéger les astronautes des tempêtes solaires dangereuses et apprendre à cultiver de la nourriture pendant les longues missions. Par exemple, des élèves peuvent faire pousser des plantes similaires à celles de la Station spatiale internationale, et les amateurs de radio peuvent construire des stations météo spatiales. Ces contributions améliorent la sécurité et la santé des futurs voyageurs de l’espace.

**Quelle est la prochaine étape** – La NASA encourage plus de personnes à rejoindre ces projets. Que vous ayez quelques minutes ou des compétences particulières, il y a une manière de participer. Avec plus de volontaires, la NASA peut collecter de meilleures données pour préparer des missions plus longues et plus lointaines.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Tout le monde peut soutenir les missions spatiales de la NASA en rejoignant des projets de science participative qui améliorent la sécurité des astronautes et aident à cultiver la nourriture dans l’espace.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo puedes ayudar a la NASA a explorar el espacio de forma segura y sostenible
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA completó recientemente su segunda misión Artemis, enviando a cuatro astronautas alrededor de la luna y de regreso. Fue el primer vuelo tripulado en el espacio profundo en más de 50 años. Aunque no todos pueden ir al espacio, la NASA ofrece formas para que personas de todo el mundo contribuyan a la exploración espacial a través de proyectos de ciencia ciudadana.

**Qué pasó** – La NASA invita a voluntarios a unirse a proyectos que apoyan los viajes humanos al espacio. Las personas han ayudado a probar variedades de plantas de chile para cultivarlas en el espacio, a estudiar la actividad solar y a analizar cómo la vida cambia en baja gravedad y alta radiación. Estos proyectos están abiertos a cualquiera interesado en la ciencia, sin importar dónde viva.

**Impacto** – Estos esfuerzos de ciencia ciudadana ayudan a la NASA a resolver problemas reales, como proteger a los astronautas de tormentas solares peligrosas y descubrir cómo cultivar alimentos durante misiones largas. Por ejemplo, estudiantes pueden cultivar plantas similares a las de la Estación Espacial Internacional, y aficionados a la radio pueden construir estaciones meteorológicas espaciales. Estas contribuciones mejoran la seguridad y salud de futuros viajeros espaciales.

**Próximo paso** – La NASA anima a más personas a unirse a estos proyectos. Ya sea que tengas unos minutos o habilidades especiales, hay una forma de participar. A medida que más voluntarios participen, la NASA podrá obtener mejores datos para prepararse para misiones más largas y profundas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Cualquiera puede apoyar las misiones espaciales de la NASA uniéndose a proyectos de ciencia ciudadana que mejoran la seguridad de los astronautas y ayudan a cultivar alimentos en el espacio.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing space garden with vibrant chili pepper plants and leafy greens growing inside a transparent lunar greenhouse under soft moonlight, surrounded by floating abstract data streams and gentle solar storm waves blending into a calm Earth horizon in the background.

articles.title: 10,000 new planets found hidden in NASA telescope data
articles.summary: NASA's Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite has been searching for exoplanets since its launch in 2018, and it turns out it may have found plenty more of them than we had thought
articles.content:
NASA's Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite has been searching for exoplanets since its launch in 2018, and it turns out it may have found plenty more of them than we had thought

An artist’s impression of a star with two planets transiting across it NASA, ESA, and G. Bacon (STScI)

Astronomers have identified more than 10,000 candidate planets in data from a NASA telescope, the most ever found in a single haul.

NASA’s Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite (TESS) was launched in 2018. It is tasked with looking at stars across the sky for planets in orbit, known as exoplanets. It identifies these exoplanets by looking for brief dips in the brightness of the light reaching Earth from each star – a sign that an exoplanet orbiting the star has passed in front of it.

So far, the telescope has found more than 750 confirmed exoplanets, but it has thousands more candidates awaiting confirmation. There are other telescopes that have found exoplanets, and the total number of exoplanets confirmed by all telescopes now stands at more than 6000.

Joshua Roth at Princeton University and his colleagues have now announced a much bigger number of possible planets by re-analysing the first year of TESS data. By combining images taken by the telescope, the researchers were able to look for planets around stars that are less bright, due to their smaller size or greater distance from Earth, than was previously possible. This revealed 11,554 candidate exoplanets, of which 10,091 have not been identified in previous exoplanet searches.

“There have been predictions that there were thousands of planets still lurking in the TESS data,” says Roth. “It just hadn’t been searched yet.”

Free newsletter Sign up to Launchpad Bring the galaxy to your inbox every month, with the latest space news, launches and astronomical occurrences from New Scientist’s Leah Crane. Sign up to newsletter

The planets extend up to 6800 light-years from Earth towards the centre of our galaxy, double the distance TESS was previously able to search. More than 90 per cent of the new planets are hot Jupiters, gas giant worlds that orbit incredibly close to their star and in just a few days. TESS is particularly suited to finding such worlds. A much smaller fraction are Neptunes and super-Earths.

However, not all of the candidates will turn out to be real planets, with each needing to be independently followed up by other telescopes. Some could be false positives, such as binary stars or other blips in the data. “TESS usually has a false positive rate of 50 per cent,” says Roth. “I would say a maximum of 5000 are real planets”, and possibly just 3000 are, he says.

Even then, the trove would increase the number of known exoplanets in the universe by half. Jessie Christiansen, chief scientist of the NASA Exoplanet Science Institute, says that will be very useful for giving us better information about where and how exoplanets form. “I want as many exoplanets as possible so that I can start slicing and dicing things,” she says. “How are they different? What kinds of different Jupiters do different stars make? These are all questions you can ask when you have a big sample.”

There are many more planets awaiting discovery in the TESS data, including about 8000 other candidate worlds identified in previous studies that still need to be examined. “We were always expecting that thousands would start flooding through at some point,” says Christiansen, with predictions suggesting TESS should find 12,000 to 15,000 confirmed planets in total. “I’ve been waiting for papers like this for a long time now.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of over 10,000 new candidate exoplanets found through re-analysis of NASA's TESS data, greatly expanding our knowledge of planets beyond our solar system. This has broad significance for astronomy and humanity's understanding of the universe, with detailed context on the discovery process and its implications.
articles.personality_title: Over 10,000 new potential planets discovered in NASA telescope data
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA launched the Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite (TESS) in 2018 to find planets outside our solar system, called exoplanets. TESS looks for tiny dips in starlight, which happen when a planet passes in front of a star.

**What happened** – Scientists re-examined the first year of data from TESS and found 11,554 new planet candidates. Most of these were not seen before. These planets are up to 6,800 light-years away, which is twice as far as TESS had looked before. Over 90% are large gas planets called hot Jupiters that orbit very close to their stars.

**Impact** – This is the largest number of planet candidates found at once and could increase the total known planets by half. It helps scientists understand how planets form and vary around different stars. Even if only some of these candidates are real, they will greatly improve our knowledge of the galaxy.

**What's next step** – Other telescopes will check these candidates to confirm which are true planets. Scientists also expect to find more planets by studying other TESS data still to be fully explored.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A fresh look at NASA’s TESS data has revealed thousands of new possible planets, expanding our view of the universe’s many worlds.
articles.personality_title_fr: Plus de 10 000 nouvelles planètes potentielles découvertes dans les données du télescope de la NASA
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA a lancé le satellite d’enquête sur les exoplanètes en transit (TESS) en 2018 pour trouver des planètes en dehors de notre système solaire, appelées exoplanètes. TESS recherche de petites baisses de la lumière des étoiles, qui se produisent lorsqu’une planète passe devant une étoile.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont réexaminé la première année de données de TESS et ont trouvé 11 554 nouvelles candidates planètes. La plupart n’avaient jamais été vues auparavant. Ces planètes se trouvent jusqu’à 6 800 années-lumière, soit deux fois plus loin que la distance explorée auparavant par TESS. Plus de 90 % sont de grandes planètes gazeuses appelées « Jupiter chauds » qui orbitent très près de leurs étoiles.

**Impact** – C’est le plus grand nombre de candidates planètes découvert en une seule fois et cela pourrait augmenter de moitié le nombre total de planètes connues. Cela aide les scientifiques à mieux comprendre comment les planètes se forment et varient autour d’étoiles différentes. Même si seulement une partie de ces candidates sont réelles, elles amélioreront grandement nos connaissances de la galaxie.

**Prochaine étape** – D’autres télescopes vont vérifier ces candidates pour confirmer lesquelles sont de vraies planètes. Les scientifiques s’attendent aussi à découvrir plus de planètes en étudiant d’autres données TESS encore à analyser.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle analyse des données de TESS de la NASA a révélé des milliers de nouvelles planètes possibles, élargissant notre vision des nombreux mondes de l’univers.
articles.personality_title_es: Descubren más de 10,000 nuevos planetas potenciales en datos del telescopio de la NASA
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – NASA lanzó el satélite de búsqueda de exoplanetas en tránsito (TESS) en 2018 para encontrar planetas fuera de nuestro sistema solar, llamados exoplanetas. TESS busca pequeñas caídas en la luz de las estrellas, que ocurren cuando un planeta pasa frente a una estrella.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos revisaron de nuevo el primer año de datos de TESS y encontraron 11,554 nuevos candidatos a planetas. La mayoría no se habían visto antes. Estos planetas están hasta a 6,800 años luz, el doble de lejos que lo que TESS había buscado antes. Más del 90% son grandes planetas gaseosos llamados Júpiter calientes que orbitan muy cerca de sus estrellas.

**Impacto** – Esta es la mayor cantidad de candidatos a planetas encontrada de una vez y podría aumentar en un 50% el número total de planetas conocidos. Ayuda a los científicos a entender mejor cómo se forman y varían los planetas alrededor de diferentes estrellas. Incluso si solo algunos de estos candidatos son reales, mejorarán mucho nuestro conocimiento de la galaxia.

**Próximo paso** – Otros telescopios revisarán estos candidatos para confirmar cuáles son planetas reales. Los científicos también esperan encontrar más planetas al estudiar otros datos de TESS que aún no se han analizado por completo.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un nuevo análisis de los datos de TESS de la NASA ha revelado miles de nuevos posibles planetas, ampliando nuestra visión de los muchos mundos del universo.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a radiant star with multiple translucent, glowing planets of varying sizes gracefully orbiting and partially eclipsing it, set against a softly colored cosmic backdrop that subtly suggests vast distances and discovery, using natural tones of gold, deep blues, and gentle oranges to evoke a sense of wonder and the unveiling of hidden worlds.

articles.title: Volunteers Help NASA Astronauts Record Lunar Flashes
articles.summary: As NASA’s Artemis II astronauts zipped around the Moon in early April, they observed flashes of light caused by meteoroids hitting the lunar surface. At the same time, volunteers for the NASA-funded Impact Flash project scanned the Moon with their own telescopes and sent their videos to scientists to share what they saw from Earth.
articles.content:
As NASA’s Artemis II astronauts zipped around the Moon in early April, they observed flashes of light caused by meteoroids hitting the lunar surface. At the same time, volunteers for the NASA-funded Impact Flash project scanned the Moon with their own telescopes and sent their videos to scientists to share what they saw from Earth.

“We were incredibly grateful for the videos people submitted,” said Impact Flash project lead Ben Fernando, a planetary scientist at Los Alamos National Laboratory. The locations and brightness of flashes observed by different instruments at different locations together can help constrain the nature and origin of the impactors, as well as the craters they form.

The Artemis II astronauts have splashed back down to Earth, so their observations of the Moon from space have come to a halt for now, but the Impact Flash team is just getting started. They need your continued help scanning the Moon to watch for flashes. If you have access to a telescope four inches in diameter or greater with video capabilities, your observations can make a difference. The more observations you submit, the better the team will be able to constrain the present-day impact rate on the Moon and how it changes over time. Instructions for making and uploading your observations can be found on the Impact Flash website.



In the future, the project team also plans to use your impact flash observations to study tremors on the Moon, similar to earthquakes. They’re called ‘moonquakes’ and they help us figure out what lies beneath the Moon’s surface.

“We are planning to send seismometers to the Moon to measure how the ground shakes,” said Fernando. “Your measurements of impact flashes will help us work out the sources of moonquakes we detect. This will help us work out what the Moon’s interior looks like.”

To collect data during the Artemis II mission, the Impact Flash investigators teamed up with several other groups of amateur astronomers, including the NASA-funded Kilo-nova Catchers, Exoplanet Watch, UNITE (Unistellar Network Investigating TESS Exoplanets), and Night Sky Network teams, as well as the Lunar Impact Flashes project, based at the National Research Council of Italy (IMATI-CNR). Thank you to all those who submitted data.

Impact Flash volunteer Joerg Tomczak sent in this image of the Moon he took during NASA’s Artemis II mission, as well as a photo of his telescope. The bright dot in the orange circle shows an impact flash candidate Credit: Joerg Tomczak

Grab your telescope and get started with Impact Flash: https://www.geodes.umd.edu/impactflash

The Impact Flash team acknowledges the work done by Institute for Applied Mathematics and Information Technologies-Consiglio Nazionale delle Ricerche (IMATI-CNR)/Italy (E. M. Alessi, M. T. Artesi) to set up the web page and A. Cook (Aberystwyth Univ., UK) and D. Koschny (Technical University of Munich, DE) for data curation. The IMATI-CNR team receives funding from the Italian Space Agency, corresponding to ESA’s (European Space Agency) Lunar Meteoroid Impacts Observer mission.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a collaborative citizen science project involving volunteers helping NASA scientists observe lunar meteoroid impacts, which advances lunar science and understanding of moonquakes. This has broad scientific significance and engages the public in meaningful space exploration activities.
articles.personality_title: Volunteers help NASA track meteoroid flashes on the Moon
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In April, NASA’s Artemis II astronauts orbited the Moon and saw bright flashes caused by tiny space rocks hitting the lunar surface. At the same time, volunteers on Earth joined a project to watch the Moon with their own telescopes.

**What happened** – The Impact Flash project, supported by NASA, invited people with telescopes to record flashes on the Moon and send videos to scientists. These volunteers helped collect videos of meteoroid impacts while astronauts observed from space. Their combined observations give scientists a clearer picture of where and how these flashes happen.

**Impact** – This teamwork between space travelers and everyday people is special because it gathers more information than astronauts alone could. Knowing where and how often meteoroids hit the Moon helps scientists learn about the Moon’s surface and even about moonquakes—shakes beneath the surface. This citizen science effort involves many groups worldwide and encourages more people to explore space from home.

**What's next step** – Now that the Artemis II astronauts have returned to Earth, the Impact Flash team wants more volunteers to keep watching the Moon. They hope to use these flash observations to better understand moonquakes by linking impacts to shaking events. Plans include sending instruments to the Moon to measure these tremors directly.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By watching lunar flashes together, NASA and volunteers worldwide are unlocking new secrets about the Moon’s surface and its hidden quakes.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des bénévoles aident la NASA à observer les éclairs lunaires causés par des météoroïdes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En avril, les astronautes de la mission Artemis II de la NASA ont orbité autour de la Lune et ont vu des éclairs lumineux causés par de petits rochers spatiaux frappant la surface lunaire. En même temps, des bénévoles sur Terre ont rejoint un projet pour observer la Lune avec leurs propres télescopes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le projet Impact Flash, soutenu par la NASA, a invité des personnes disposant de télescopes à enregistrer ces éclairs sur la Lune et à envoyer des vidéos aux scientifiques. Ces bénévoles ont aidé à collecter des vidéos d’impacts de météoroïdes pendant que les astronautes observaient depuis l’espace. Ces observations combinées donnent aux scientifiques une image plus claire de l’endroit et de la fréquence de ces éclairs.

**Impact** – Cette collaboration entre astronautes et citoyens est unique car elle permet de rassembler plus d’informations que les seuls astronautes. Savoir où et à quelle fréquence les météoroïdes frappent la Lune aide les scientifiques à mieux comprendre la surface lunaire et même les « sismos lunaires », des secousses sous la surface. Ce projet de science participative réunit plusieurs groupes dans le monde et encourage davantage de personnes à explorer l’espace depuis chez eux.

**La prochaine étape** – Maintenant que les astronautes d’Artemis II sont revenus sur Terre, l’équipe Impact Flash souhaite que plus de bénévoles continuent à observer la Lune. Ils espèrent utiliser ces observations d’éclairs pour mieux comprendre les sismos lunaires en reliant les impacts aux secousses détectées. Ils prévoient aussi d’envoyer des instruments sur la Lune pour mesurer ces tremblements directement.

**En une phrase** – En observant ensemble les éclairs lunaires, la NASA et des bénévoles du monde entier découvrent de nouveaux secrets sur la surface et les tremblements cachés de la Lune.
articles.personality_title_es: Voluntarios ayudan a la NASA a registrar destellos de impactos en la Luna
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En abril, los astronautas de la misión Artemis II de la NASA orbitaban la Luna y vieron destellos brillantes causados por pequeñas rocas espaciales que chocaban con la superficie lunar. Al mismo tiempo, voluntarios en la Tierra participaron en un proyecto para observar la Luna con sus propios telescopios.

**Qué pasó** – El proyecto Impact Flash, financiado por la NASA, invitó a personas con telescopios a grabar los destellos en la Luna y enviar videos a los científicos. Estos voluntarios ayudaron a recopilar videos de los impactos de meteoroides mientras los astronautas observaban desde el espacio. Las observaciones combinadas ofrecen a los científicos una imagen más clara de dónde y con qué frecuencia ocurren estos destellos.

**Impacto** – Esta colaboración entre viajeros espaciales y personas comunes es especial porque recoge más información de la que podrían obtener solo los astronautas. Saber dónde y con qué frecuencia los meteoroides golpean la Luna ayuda a los científicos a aprender sobre su superficie e incluso sobre los “sismos lunares”, movimientos bajo la superficie. Este esfuerzo de ciencia ciudadana involucra a varios grupos en todo el mundo y anima a más personas a explorar el espacio desde casa.

**Siguiente paso** – Ahora que los astronautas de Artemis II regresaron a la Tierra, el equipo de Impact Flash quiere que más voluntarios sigan observando la Luna. Esperan usar estas observaciones para entender mejor los sismos lunares relacionando los impactos con los movimientos detectados. También planean enviar instrumentos a la Luna para medir estos temblores directamente.

**Una frase clave** – Al observar juntos los destellos lunares, la NASA y voluntarios de todo el mundo están descubriendo nuevos secretos sobre la superficie y los temblores ocultos de la Luna.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing telescope pointed at a softly illuminated crescent Moon, with subtle bright flashes appearing on the lunar surface, surrounded by a calm night sky filled with gentle stars and faint outlines of distant observers represented by silhouetted hands adjusting telescope lenses.
